hero_summary: Nairuz is a secondary maqam inside the Rast family. It is not best understood as an independent maqam outside Rast, but rather as a Rast-type pathway in which Bayati on the fifth becomes the dominant secondary route.
short_identity: A Rast-family branch built on Jins Rast on Rast (C), with Bayati on Nawa (G) as its most important secondary jins.

quick_facts.common_start: It usually begins by confirming Rast first, then ascends to Bayati on the fifth and makes that upper route clear
quick_facts.character: Basically Rast in identity, but softer and more inclined toward Bayati on the fifth in the upper region

sayr.summary: Nairuz begins from Jins Rast on Rast (C), clearly confirming the tonic and the original jins. It then ascends toward Nawa (G), but instead of opening Hijaz on the fifth as in Suznak, or keeping plain Rast on the fifth as in basic Rast, it stabilizes Bayati on the fifth and makes that the dominant secondary path. It may then reach Kardan (upper C) before descending back to Rast (C). Nairuz is therefore understood primarily as a Rast-based melodic path in which Bayati on the fifth is highlighted with clarity.
resting_tones: Rast (C): the final tonic, because the maqam remains within the Rast family
Nawa (G): the most important secondary point of rest, because it carries the Bayati jins that distinguishes Nairuz
Kardan (upper C): a natural upper summit after Bayati on the fifth has been established
motion_notes: Nairuz is distinguished through its secondary jins on the fifth
If Hijaz on the fifth dominates, the ear moves toward Suznak; if Rast on the fifth dominates, the ear returns toward basic Rast
Nairuz is very rare as a fully independent maqam, but very prominent as a melodic path inside Rast and Suznak
Analytically, the better question is often not ‘is the whole piece Nairuz?’ but ‘does a clear Nairuz-like pathway appear here?’
common_path: Establish Bayati on the fifth and build the main phrases there
Reach Kardan (upper C) if needed at the peak

common_modulations: Bayati on Nawa (G) as the dominant secondary center
Nairuz may appear inside Rast or Suznak because of the close relation between Bayati on the fifth and Hijaz on the fifth
If Hijaz on the fifth becomes frequent, the sound approaches Suznak
If Rast on the fifth becomes frequent, the sound approaches basic Rast

examples.title: Ya Hilalan
examples.note: Perhaps the best known example of Nairuz, even though it appears inside a Rast wasla context

examples.title: Asl al-gharam
examples.note: An important example because, while named under Rast/Suznak labels, it uses a very prominent Nairuz pathway

examples.title: Ghanni li shwayya shwayya
examples.note: A famous example in which extended performances pass through sections where a genuine Nairuz manifestation becomes clear

reference_recordings.title: Ya Hilalan
reference_recordings.note: A key reference for hearing Nairuz as a clear Rast branch

reference_recordings.title: Mubarraq' al-jamal
reference_recordings.note: An example of Maqam Yakah, the older form historically close to Nairuz

reference_recordings.title: Asl al-gharam
reference_recordings.note: An important reference for understanding how Nairuz lives inside Rast and Suznak more than as an isolated independent maqam

reference_recordings.title: Ghanni li shwayya shwayya
reference_recordings.note: A good reference for hearing Bayati on the fifth inside a wider Suznak/Rast space
